title: Spaghetti aglio, olio, e peperoncino
subtitle: Garlic, oil, chilli, and parsley, Rome's midnight pasta
excerpt: Spaghetti aglio, olio, e peperoncino is five ingredients, one pan, and twenty minutes. The sauce is an emulsion of pasta water and good olive oil, with garlic cooked slowly to gold and chilli for heat. Parsley at the end. The simplicity is the point: every element has to be right.
sourceNotes:
• The dish is central to the Neapolitan and Roman pasta tradition and predates any single codified source. Synthesis draws on Marcella Hazan (*Essentials of Classic Italian Cooking*, 1992) and the broader Neapolitan cookery tradition documented in Ada Boni (*Il Talismano della Felicità*, 1927).
• The emulsification technique using pasta water is the key technical contribution documented by Italian food writers from the mid-twentieth century onwards.

content.text: Five ingredients, one pan, twenty minutes.

is the Roman answer to an empty fridge: a sauce made from pantry staples that asks very little of the cook. The trick is in the garlic. It goes in cold oil and heats slowly, colouring to gold without crossing into bitter brown. The moment it is right, pasta water goes in to stop the cooking, and the oil and water emulsify around the starch into something silky rather than greasy.
Use good olive oil. The oil is the sauce here, and there is nowhere for a flat or bitter oil to hide. The amount looks like a lot. It is. This is not a dish where you drizzle.

items.name: Chilli flakes
items.prepNote: or one fresh red chilli, deseeded and sliced

content.text: Wide frying pan and large saucepan. The frying pan needs to be big enough to toss the pasta in at the end.
Bring a large saucepan of water to a rolling boil. Salt generously. Drop in {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti. Cook for 1 minute less than the packet time. It will finish cooking in the oil.

While the pasta cooks, pour {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil into a cold 26 cm frying pan. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes. Set the pan over a medium-low heat. As the oil heats, the garlic will begin to sizzle gently. Watch it closely: you want it pale gold, not brown. This takes 4 to 6 minutes from cold. The moment the garlic is lightly coloured, pull the pan off the heat.

Scoop out a large mug of pasta water (about 200 ml) just before the pasta is ready. Drain the pasta. Return the frying pan to a medium heat and pour in about 100 ml of the pasta water. It will spit and sizzle. Add the drained pasta to the pan and toss vigorously with tongs for 1 to 2 minutes. The oil and water should emulsify into a light, glossy coating around the strands. Add more pasta water if needed to loosen.
Off the heat, toss in {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley. Serve immediately. No cheese, this is one of the few Italian pastas where parmesan is out of place.

items.fix: Always start in cold oil over medium-low heat. If it burns, start again, bitter garlic cannot be corrected. A new batch takes 5 minutes.
items.cause: The heat was too high or the garlic was added to a hot pan.
items.symptom: Garlic has gone brown and bitter

items.fix: Add pasta water in stages while tossing hard and continuously. The starch in the water is what enables the emulsion. The tossing motion is what creates it.
items.cause: Too little pasta water, or the pasta was not tossed vigorously enough.
items.symptom: Sauce is greasy and the oil has not emulsified

items.fix: Move the pasta directly from the colander to the pan. If it must wait, add a small splash of pasta water to the colander and toss.
items.cause: Left to sit in the colander after draining.
items.symptom: Pasta has clumped together before going in the pan

items.fix: Slice the garlic as thinly as possible. The more surface area in contact with the oil, the more flavour is released. Infusing for the full 4 to 6 minutes is also important.
items.cause: The garlic was not given enough time to infuse the oil, or the slices were too thick.
items.symptom: Not enough garlic flavour in the finished dish

content.text: Aglio, olio, e peperoncino is part of the Neapolitan and Roman cucina povera tradition: food made from what the pantry always has, with skill substituting for expense. In Rome it is eaten late at night, after the theatre or cinema, quick to make, satisfying, and requiring no trip to the shops. It has been adopted across Italy as the standard answer to the question of what to cook when there is nothing in the house. The answer, it turns out, is usually pasta and olive oil.
